title: New business at the Lakeside Association
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the monthly meeting of the Lakeside Community Association. The chair turns to new business.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We'll now move to new business.

turns.speaker: Member (Ramos)
turns.line: I move that the Association spend $500 on new signage.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Before I do anything, what needs to happen with this motion?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what must happen before this main motion is debated?
turns.gold: A main motion requires a second before it is considered. The chair should call for a second; if seconded, the chair states the question and opens it to debate.

ref.quote: The basic requirement for approval of an action or choice by a deliberative assembly, except where a rule provides otherwise, is a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: It is moved and seconded to spend $500 on new signage. Is there any debate?

turns.speaker: Member (Park)
turns.line: I move to amend by striking out '$500' and inserting '$750'.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is this amendment handled, and what vote does it need?
turns.gold: The amendment needs a second and must be germane to the motion. It is debatable and is adopted by a majority vote — even though the main motion might require a different threshold, an amendment needs only a majority.

ref.quote: A secondary motion thus takes precedence over the main motion; and a main motion takes precedence over nothing and yields to all applicable secondary motions.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second the amendment!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The amendment carries. The motion now reads 'spend $750 on new signage.' Further debate?

turns.speaker: Member (Ramos)
turns.line: We've heard enough — I move the previous question!
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is the motion for the Previous Question handled?
turns.gold: The Previous Question needs a second, is not debatable, and requires a two-thirds vote to adopt. If it passes, debate ends immediately and the assembly votes on the pending motion.

ref.quote: The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.

turns.speaker: Member (Chen)
turns.line: Second!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The previous question is adopted by more than two-thirds; debate is closed. (to you) What vote is now needed to adopt the main motion itself?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what vote adopts this main motion?
turns.gold: A main motion is adopted by a majority vote unless a special rule or the bylaws require more. Here a majority of the votes cast decides it.

ref.quote: The basic requirement for approval of an action or choice by a deliberative assembly, except where a rule provides otherwise, is a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The motion is adopted. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: A thin house at the Booster Club
setting: The Booster Club meeting is scheduled to start, but only a few members are present. A member is eager to get going.

turns.speaker: Member (Doyle)
turns.line: There aren't many of us, but let's just start adopting motions.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what must be confirmed before the assembly transacts business?
turns.gold: Business cannot be transacted without a quorum present. The chair must confirm that a quorum, as defined by the bylaws, is present before the assembly takes any substantive action.

ref.quote: A quorum in an assembly is the number of members … who must be present in order that business can be validly transacted.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Thank you. Members have arrived and we now have a quorum. Proceed.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: Point of order! I don't think the last speaker was recognized.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how is a Point of Order handled?
turns.gold: A Point of Order needs no second, is not debatable, and is raised at the time of the breach. The chair rules on the point; the chair's ruling may be challenged by an Appeal.

ref.quote: When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or "raise a question of order," as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) I'm inclined to rule the point well taken — but should I make this ruling, or should you as the parliamentarian?
turns.prompt: Advise: who rules on the point — you or the chair?
turns.gold: The chair rules, not the parliamentarian. The parliamentarian only advises the chair (usually privately); the chair then states the ruling to the assembly.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian is a consultant, commonly a professional, who advises the president and other officers, committees, and members on matters of parliamentary procedure.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Understood. I rule the point well taken.

title: A close election at the Arts Guild
setting: The Arts Guild is electing its president. The bylaws require a ballot vote but are silent on the threshold. Three candidates are running.

turns.speaker: Teller
turns.line: First ballot: Alvarez 40, Brooks 35, Cho 20. Alvarez has the most votes.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Alvarez has a plurality. Can I declare Alvarez elected?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: is a plurality enough to elect here?
turns.gold: No. Unless the bylaws provide otherwise, election requires a majority — more than half of the votes cast. A plurality does not elect.

ref.quote: A plurality vote is the largest number of votes to be given any candidate or proposition when three or more choices are possible; the candidate or proposition receiving the largest number of votes has a plurality.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We balloted again: Alvarez 48, Brooks 42, Cho 5 — still no one over half. (to you) What do we do now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what happens when no candidate has a majority?
turns.gold: Balloting is simply repeated until one candidate receives a majority. Candidates are not dropped unless the bylaws so provide, and members may change their votes on each ballot.

ref.quote: A plurality vote is the largest number of votes to be given any candidate or proposition when three or more choices are possible; the candidate or proposition receiving the largest number of votes has a plurality.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Third ballot: Alvarez has 51 of 95 votes — a majority. Alvarez is elected.

title: A dues increase at the Historical Society
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Historical Society. A bylaw amendment to raise annual dues from $50 to $75 was submitted in writing at last month's regular meeting, and is now up for consideration.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Next: the bylaw amendment to raise dues from $50 to $75, noticed last month.

turns.speaker: Member (Vance)
turns.line: Let's just pass it with a simple majority and move on.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what vote does adopting this bylaw amendment require?
turns.gold: Amending the bylaws takes a two-thirds vote, and it is in order now only because previous notice was given at the last regular meeting. A simple majority is not sufficient.

ref.quote: These bylaws may be amended at any regular meeting of the Society by a two-thirds vote, provided that the amendment has been submitted in writing at the previous regular meeting.

turns.speaker: Member (Ruiz)
turns.line: I move to amend the amendment — make the new dues $150 instead of $75.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: is amending the figure up to $150 in order?
turns.gold: No. An amendment to a bylaw amendment cannot exceed the scope of the notice. Members were noticed of an increase to $75, so a higher figure like $150 is out of order; a figure between the current $50 and the noticed $75 would be in order.

ref.quote: If the bylaws require previous notice for their amendment … no amendment to a bylaw amendment is in order that increases the modification of the article or provision to be amended.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The amendment to $150 is out of order — it exceeds the scope of the notice. We'll vote on the $75 amendment as noticed.
